name: Calella to the Punta de la Tordera
name_original: De Calella a la Punta de la Tordera
description:
From Calella to the mouth of the Tordera, this bike route offers you to travel the long stretch of coast that joins the towns of Calella, Pineda de Mar, Santa Susanna and Malgrat de Mar, in the Maresme region of Barcelona. Delimited by the promontory where the Calella lighthouse is located to the south and the Tordera delta to the north, on this coastal walk you always pedal along the bike path. During the journey you can take advantage of both the beaches and the services that you find along the Route: from bars and restaurants to beach sports activities. This Route can be done with almost any type of bicycle, since the path is paved or is a dirt track in good condition.

Departs from Calella, on the Garbí beach, just below the Calella lighthouse. You cycle along the bike path to reach the next beach, known as Platja Gran de Calella. Almost without realizing it, you leave Calella to enter Pineda de Mar, where two more beaches await you: known as Platja dels Pescadors and Platja dels Pins. The seafront of Santa Susanna, which is the one you will find below, is different from that of Calella and Pineda de Mar, more touristy and urbanized. Farms predominate here and the area is quieter.

When you arrive at Malgrat de Mar you return to a busier coastline. But as you get closer to the Tordera river and its delta, the tall buildings disappear and once again the beach is flanked by campsites and agricultural areas. In order to reach the mouth of the Tordera by bike paths, the Route goes briefly inland to take you right up to the Tordera delta. Here there is a bird observation post from where you can observe the varied avian fauna of the area.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Calella offer a diverse landscape blending coastal and mountainous terrain. The region features a rugged coastline with panoramic views of the Mediterranean Sea, alongside inland areas characterized by lush forests and rolling hills of the Montnegre i el Corredor Natural Park. This combination provides varied cycling experiences, from flat coastal paths to more challenging routes through natural parkland. Best touring cycling routes around Calella The most popular touring cycling route is Calella to the Punta de la Tordera, a 9.0 miles (14.5 km) trail that takes 53 minutes to complete. This easy route offers coastal views along the Mediterranean. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Balí Beach – Playa de Ocata loop from Calella, a moderate 46.5 miles (74.9 km) path. This route follows the coastline, passing through various beach areas. Local touring cyclists also love the Castle Park – Punta del Tordera Beach loop from Calella, a 21.4 miles (34.4 km) trail leading through coastal areas and parks, often completed in about 2 hours 3 minutes. Touring cycling around Calella is defined by coastal paths, lush forests, and rolling hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging routes. The routes in Calella are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 140 reviews. More than 2,500 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Calella's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Calella?
A: Calella offers a diverse selection of over 40 touring cycling routes. These routes cater to various skill levels, blending coastal paths with more challenging trails through natural parkland.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Calella?
A: Yes, Calella has 7 easy touring cycling routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a relaxed ride. A great option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1219926442/calella-to-the-punta-de-la-tordera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calella to the Punta de la Tordera&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 9-mile trail offering pleasant coastal views.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Calella?
A: The terrain around Calella is quite varied, offering both coastal and mountainous experiences. You'll find routes that hug the Mediterranean coastline, providing panoramic sea views, as well as inland trails through the lush forests and rolling hills of the Montnegre i el Corredor Natural Park. Some routes may feature unpaved segments and significant elevation gains, particularly in the park.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Calella features 16 difficult routes. One such route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40346215" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collsacreu – Garbí Beach loop from Calella&lt;/a&gt;, which covers approximately 23.8 miles (38.3 km) and includes over 500 meters of elevation gain, offering a demanding ride through varied landscapes.

Q: Can I find family-friendly touring cycling routes in Calella?
A: While specific family-friendly routes with child-specific information are not detailed, the easy coastal paths are generally suitable for families. The town's parks like Parc Dalmau and Parc Central also offer green spaces for relaxation and light outdoor activities, which can be combined with shorter, less strenuous rides.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Calella are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/32074930" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle Park – Punta del Tordera Beach loop from Calella&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 21.4-mile (34.4 km) trail that takes you through coastal areas and parks, bringing you back to your starting point.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Along the routes, you can discover various natural and historical landmarks. Highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5926463" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mouth of the Tordera&lt;/a&gt;, the historical &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2558847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Three Borders Cross&lt;/a&gt;, and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6190693" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lookout Point of Turo Gros de Miralles&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter the ruins of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3027059" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montpalau Castle&lt;/a&gt;, offering glimpses into the region's past.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Calella?
A: Calella is recognized as a Sports Tourism Destination, making it suitable for cycling for much of the year. The Mediterranean climate generally offers pleasant conditions. Spring and autumn typically provide mild temperatures ideal for touring, while summer can be warm, especially for inland routes. Coastal rides might be more comfortable during hotter months due to sea breezes.

Q: How can I access touring cycling routes using public transport or find parking?
A: Calella is well-connected, and its status as a cycling-friendly town suggests good infrastructure. While specific parking locations for each route are not detailed, the town's accessibility by public transport (trains often run along the coast) makes it convenient to reach starting points. Many routes begin directly from Calella, simplifying access.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Calella?
A: The touring cycling routes in Calella are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 140 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from stunning coastal views to the tranquility of the Montnegre i el Corredor Natural Park, and the variety of routes available for all abilities.

Q: Are there any long-distance touring cycling options from Calella?
A: Yes, Calella's location within the Maresme region and its proximity to Barcelona allow for longer touring cycling adventures. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40349753" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balí Beach – Playa de Ocata loop from Calella&lt;/a&gt;, spanning nearly 46.5 miles (75 km), demonstrate the potential for extended rides that follow the coastline and pass through various beach areas and towns.

Q: Are there cafes or amenities along the touring cycling routes?
A: Given Calella's coastal location and the routes passing through charming towns and beach areas, you can expect to find cafes, restaurants, and other amenities along many of the touring cycling routes, especially those closer to the coast or urban centers. The Montnegre i el Corredor Natural Park might have fewer options, so planning ahead for water and snacks is advisable for inland routes.
tips:
Garbí beach is located in the southern part of the municipality of Calella, in front of the hotel zone. It is delimited by the promenade of the same name, and includes different services, such as two beach soccer fields, nine beach volleyball fields, showers, surveillance and rescue service, bike lane, security and surveillance services (local police and Cruz Red) and public services adapted for disabled people.

It has received the Q for quality from the Institute for Spanish Tourist Quality and is a Blue Flag for the beaches of Europe.
This bike path runs along the Garbí beaches and the Gran de Calella beach. It has parking spaces for bicycles.

This beach in the municipality of Santa Susanna is characterized by having small mounds of rocks that divide the beach into various coves. It has basic services, such as access for the disabled, toilets or parking.

Punta del Tordera beach is located in front of the Malgrat de Mar camping area and reaches the mouth of the Tordera river.

It has services such as toilets for people with disabilities, an aid station, showers and an amphibious chair for people with reduced mobility to bathe.

The mouth of the Tordera forms a natural delta thanks to the sediments carried by the river. After the Gloria storm in 2020 and thanks to the massive influx of sediments, the delta experienced the greatest growth in recent years, when a large sand bar formed that goes 300 meters into the sea and has created a huge inner lagoon.

The Delta de la Tordera has been declared a Protected Natural Area, and is included in the Natura 2000 Network. The landscape of the delta shows mainly swampy vegetation and riparian forest, including reedbeds. You can also find numerous animal species, such as the leper pond turtle, the southern frog, the spur toad, the collared snake, or birds such as thrushes, gray herons, kingfishers and others.
